risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Students were then randomized to experimental or control groups, using the random blocks method21 stratifying on gender (male/female) and self-reported attention deficit hyperactivity disorder status (yes, no), to either (a) immediate instruction in the TM program or (b) wait list/delayed start condition." Comment: The study reports a blocked randomization procedure with stratification, which indicates a systematic random allocation process rather than a non-random or predictable method. Although the exact random-number source is not stated in this excerpt, the description is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as low risk under a permissive standard.

Quote: "The treatment group allocations were concealed by the study statistician, with individual treatment group assignments revealed to the project manager only when study participants completed baseline testing." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment procedure that prevented the recruiter from knowing the upcoming assignment before enrollment/baseline completion, so allocation concealment is low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls" and "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering the TM intervention could not have been blinded in a TM vs wait-list trial, and the intervention involved extensive instruction and follow-up meetings. Because the main outcomes included self-reported psychological distress and coping, and even blood pressure could be influenced by participant behavior/expectation, knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect outcomes. Therefore the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Quote: "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Quote: "BP was measured in a seated position, with an automated BP monitor ... using standard clinical trial technique." Comment: The outcome assessors (research staff) were explicitly masked to allocation, and the main outcomes were measured by masked staff using an automated blood pressure monitor; psychological outcomes were self-reported but were also collected by masked assessors. This makes detection bias unlikely.

Quote: "A limitation is that about 30% of the pretest sample was unavailable for post-testing. This concern is mitigated by the fact that across groups completers did not differ from non-completers on any baseline entry characteristics. Also there were no differences between groups on baseline primary and secondary outcomes in noncompleters." Comment: Although overall attrition was around 30%, the paper reports that completers and non-completers were similar at baseline and that there was no evidence of differential dropout by group on baseline outcomes. The analysis was based on participants with baseline and post-test data, and the reported attrition does not appear obviously imbalanced or outcome-related, so bias from missing data is unlikely.
